content: Let’s cut through the noise about synchronization primitives and get straight to why `thread::Barrier` is often the right tool in your belt, but only if you understand exactly when it belongs. Think of a barrier as a digital meeting point where N threads must arrive before anyone can leave; it’s not for counting down like `countdown`, nor is it for locking shared state like a mutex would be. You use `thread::Barrier` to enforce a global pause in an execution stream, effectively serializing a parallel phase into a single logical step before handing control back to the next batch of work. Imagine you’re building a pipeline processor where each stage needs all previous stages finished before starting; without a barrier, Stage 2 might fire off before Stage 1 completes, leading to race conditions or corrupted intermediate data. The concrete pattern here is simple: spawn N worker threads that perform identical independent tasks, pass them the same `Barrier`, have them execute their logic, then call `barrier.wait()`. Only after all threads hit that line does execution proceed to the next phase, perhaps aggregating results into a vector or updating a channel. This approach shines when you need strict ordering across multiple workers—say, in a distributed simulation where every node must commit its local state before the global system transitions to the next tick. The key distinction from other synchronization primitives is that `thread::Barrier` does not protect individual variables; it protects the timeline itself. If you try to use it when threads need to read and write shared mutable data, you’ll immediately hit a deadlock because no thread can proceed past the wait until everyone arrives, yet if any thread tries to access shared memory before the barrier clears, that’s undefined behavior. So my recommendation is always: use `thread::Barrier` for phased computation where every worker does the same thing independently and must finish in lockstep, but never reach for it when you have fine-grained locking requirements or need to protect specific data structures; those cases belong to `Mutex`, `RwLock`, or `Arc<Mutex<T>>`. In your next design review, ask yourself: does every thread need to synchronize at this exact moment regardless of individual progress? If yes, pull out a barrier. If no, drop it and go back to fine-grained locking.
